title: Two Flowers
description: Shimamura runs into Adachi's mother at the gym pool. A conversation about names, similarities, and family—with a touch of teasing and a rare moment of connection.
content:
Ah.

Yah-hoo, Sakura~

I ran into her in the parking lot outside the gym, so I called out to her—and got a sour look in return.

I told you already, that's my daughter's name.

Oh, right.

I've always been bad at remembering names. Mostly because I rarely get to call anyone by name in the first place, y'know?

Right?

What are you asking me for?

She always talks like her sentences have barbs on them—like she treats everything in front of her with suspicion. In a way, that negativity resembles Adachi herself, even if it's a little different.

Well then, I'll be going.

Let's go.

When I saw her heading inside the gym alone, I grabbed her arm and pulled her forward.

What now?

What was your name again?

I asked this to show I wasn't trying to call her by the wrong name. She stared at the arm I was holding, then let out a dramatic sigh.

Even if I tell you, you'll just forget again.

You can't give up before trying. That makes me look bad.

I already tried. That's why I gave up.

I did remember until the time we went home separately, you know.

After that, I had too many things to think about, so the name just slipped away. But since we always meet when I come to the gym, it'll be fine. Probably.

While pulling her forward, I noticed her legs were a little longer than mine. She was also taller, but that part doesn't count as losing. Make no mistake.

How have things been? Getting along with your daughter?

No change. Same as always.

Mhm, a steady relationship—not a bad thing at all.

…You really are unbelievable.

Yep~

No, she was not complimenting me. But she also stopped trying to pull her arm away—guess she gave up on resisting in every sense.

Well, whatever happens with us, as long as our daughters get along, that's enough.

When she switched the topic to her daughter, the downward curve of her annoyed mouth softened just a little— like a tiny gap opened up. She probably didn't even notice it herself.

Whenever my daughter is with your daughter, she always looks so happy.

That's why I think meeting her must've been a real blessing.

Hearing that, she said:

My daughter too. It seems yours has helped her a lot… though I'm not sure exactly what.

Why add that second half like she's trying to distance herself?

Putting up walls in places where there's no need… it's so—well, so Adachi-ish.

Staaaaare.

What.

You really are a lot like your daughter.

I'd felt that clearly when talking to her daughter too. Maybe social interaction is just difficult for them fundamentally. But even so, I can't help but talk to them—because that's just who I am.

Yeah… although being like her isn't exactly something to be proud of. She sighed, sounding like she was speaking her true feelings.

People say my daughter and I are half alike, half not.

…I think you're similar.

Really?

It's true—sometimes my own daughter, Hougetsu, gets stubborn about the strangest things.

At the gym counter, we exchanged our membership cards for locker keys. Since the numbers weren't assigned, we ended up with adjacent lockers. She made the most obvious 'Ugh, why me' face imaginable.

Besties!

Does saying stuff like that not embarrass you? No, wait, you definitely don't feel embarrassment.

She solved her own question before I could answer. Honestly, I admire how carefree she is.

Let's work up a good sweat today!

I'm not doing anything with you.

Let's go together!

You're so annoying…

Her lower lip quivered like she'd just tasted something bitter. Seeing that gave me an idea.

Oh, I remember now.

Huh?

Akka.

Her eyes froze in surprise—yep, nailed it.

It's a beautiful name. I like it.

When I casually complimented it, her cheeks didn't turn red like a flower blooming—Nope. She just frowned and said coldly: If you like it so much, stop forgetting it every time.

Isn't it great that I love it no matter how many times I hear it?

As I laughed out loud, she looked like she'd finally— finally—given up on resisting me, and she gave a tiny smile.

Talking to you is exhausting.

I hear that a lot.

But I'm always full of energy—maybe I absorb the youthfulness around me.

Akka, are you heading straight to the pool today?

I only asked so I could keep teasing her, but she froze again.

What's wrong?

Nothing.

She turned her face away.

…I am going swimming, though.

Oh, really.

The moment I responded casually, she shot me a death glare. What did I do??

…Yoshika.

She muttered my name in a low, threatening voice.

It felt like she was warning me I'd pushed some invisible button—I had no idea what I did, but the intimidation was real.

Come to think of it, has she ever called me by name before?

I don't remember at all, but hearing it made me weirdly happy.

You remembered my name instantly—very smart of you.

Are you making fun of me?

Not at aaaaall~

…And I almost got smacked for that one.
translatorNote: The title refers to the names of Adachi and her mother: Adachi Akka (安達赤華, 'red flower') and Adachi Sakura (安達桜, 'cherry blossom'). By contrast, Shimamura Yoshika (島村良香) can be read as 'good fragrance.'
